era_context: Identical to -2250 and -2251 BCE: Akkadian Empire under Naram-Sin at its territorial peak; Old Kingdom Egypt fragmenting; Indus Valley Civilization in mature Harappan phase; Early Bronze Age II in the Aegean.

events.title: Akkadian Empire at Territorial Zenith
events.description: Naram-Sin's Akkadian Empire reaches its maximum territorial extent. The empire controls Mesopotamia, parts of the Levant, the Zagros Mountains, and trade routes to the Persian Gulf and beyond. This represents the apex of Akkadian power before internal strain and external pressure begin to erode imperial control. Administrative systems are in place to govern this vast territory, though the strain of maintaining such an extensive empire is becoming evident.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Akkadian chronology and territorial extent

events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's reign and the general extent of Akkadian power are well-attested. The specific dating of the zenith of power to this particular year is conventional rather than documented.

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization: Continued Urban Development
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization continues its mature phase. Urban centres maintain their characteristic features: standardized brick sizes, grid-planned streets, sophisticated drainage systems, and evidence of craft specialization. Long-distance trade networks remain active. The undeciphered Indus script continues to appear on seals and pottery, suggesting a literate administrative class, though the script's meaning remains unknown.
events.certainty_note: The Harappan mature phase is archaeologically well-established. Specific events of individual years are not documented.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. The year -2252 BCE is not marked by any known specific historical events in surviving records.
historiographic_note: The year -2252 BCE is not individually documented in any known historical source. The entries above represent the general context of the era. Year-by-year specificity is essentially absent for this period; historical knowledge is reconstructed from longer-term trends.

graph_edges.note: Part of the same period of Akkadian imperial expansion.
